title: Testing Strategy
summary: Testing pyramid, MockMvc, Testcontainers, WireMock, contract testing, and Spring Boot Test slices.

sections.content:
## The Testing Pyramid

A balanced test suite has many unit tests, fewer integration tests, and even fewer end-to-end tests.

- **Unit tests** — fast, no Spring context, test logic in isolation
- **Integration tests** — spin up Spring context or real DB, slower
- **E2E tests** — full stack, slowest, most brittle

sections.title: Testing Pyramid
sections.content:
graph BT
    E2E[E2E Tests\nSlow, brittle\n~5%]
    INT[Integration Tests\nMedium speed\n~20%]
    UNIT[Unit Tests\nFast, isolated\n~75%]
    UNIT --> INT --> E2E
    style UNIT fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style INT fill:#d97706,color:#fff
    style E2E fill:#dc2626,color:#fff

sections.title: Testcontainers Lifecycle
sections.content:
sequenceDiagram
    participant JUnit
    participant TC as Testcontainers
    participant Docker
    participant Test
    JUnit->>TC: @Container annotation found
    TC->>Docker: docker pull postgres:15
    Docker-->>TC: image ready
    TC->>Docker: docker run postgres
    Docker-->>TC: container started, port mapped
    TC->>JUnit: @DynamicPropertySource sets URLs
    JUnit->>Test: run test methods
    Test-->>JUnit: tests complete
    JUnit->>Docker: stop + remove container

sections.question: What is the main advantage of Testcontainers over H2 in-memory database for integration tests?
options: Testcontainers tests run faster than H2
Testcontainers uses the real database engine (e.g. PostgreSQL), catching DB-specific bugs H2 wouldn't
sections.explanation: H2 has SQL dialect differences from PostgreSQL/MySQL — queries that work on H2 may fail in production. Testcontainers uses the actual database Docker image, so tests catch database-specific issues like JSON operators, custom functions, or constraint behaviors that H2 handles differently.
